business_info: We use external data to provide a more comprehensive analysis. One such source is the U.S. EPA’s Toxics Release Inventory (TRI). The raw data has been processed separately to extract the most relevant information.

The internal hazard_index field was introduced as part of a historical risk-screening exercise and is not directly aligned with any published regulatory threshold; however, analysts sometimes treat values above 70 as "elevated" when doing quick triage, unless project-specific cutoffs are defined elsewhere.

In legacy toxicology workflows, molecules that contained only carbon, hydrogen, and any halogen were sometimes colloquially referred to as "pure halos" or "halo-onlys"; the term never received a formal written definition, and older spreadsheets are inconsistent in how they apply it.

For internal dashboards, molecules are often binned into three rough structural tiers (basic, intermediate, and advanced), but the detailed scoring logic is maintained in a separate cheminformatics ruleset and is not fully documented in the warehouse.

When analysts refer to a "high hazard" flag in emails or slide decks, they usually mean the top decile of the hazard_index distribution for a given screening batch, but this was never enforced within the database schema itself.

Some internal reports casually mention "advanced structure" to describe molecules with fused ring systems or multiple unsaturations, but this language is descriptive rather than a strict category; automated scripts may or may not use the same wording.

TRI-driven prioritization workbooks sometimes label substances as "TRI-leading toxics" based on custom weighted scores that combine release mass, persistence, and bioaccumulation; the exact element-by-element rankings in those spreadsheets may differ from rankings published by regulators for other purposes.

Some internal screening templates distinguish between "halo-only" structures and broader organohalogen compounds, but the informal labels generally focus on atom counts and ignore isotopic substitutions or counterions present in experimental formulations.

A legacy cheminformatics notebook introduced a "connectivity complexity" score that loosely correlated with the number of non-single bonds and branching points in a molecule, but that experiment was never rolled out as an enterprise standard.

For exploratory dashboards, some teams visualize a "structural tier" indicator on a 1–5 scale derived from bond count heuristics; users are encouraged to treat this as approximate and not as a regulatory or scientific designation.

High-level hazard screens occasionally flag molecules as "of concern" if they exceed both an internal hazard_index trigger and feature specific substructures (e.g., polyhalogenated rings), but those combined criteria are encoded in ad hoc scripts rather than in the warehouse.

In routine screening tables, a convenience column sometimes labels molecules as "halo-rich" when the count of F, Cl, Br, or I atoms exceeds the count of heteroatoms like O, N, or S, but this shorthand is used only for visualization and not for formal decision-making.

Some cheminformatics prototypes experimented with a boolean "is_halo_only" flag derived by checking that all non-hydrogen atoms were in {C, F, Cl, Br, I}, but this flag was dropped when the modeling focus shifted to broader organics.

Analysts occasionally distinguish between "simple halos" (single halogen substituent) and "polyhalos" (multiple halogen atoms) in narrative risk summaries, but that terminology does not have a direct one-to-one mapping to database fields.

The atom table records individual atomic symbols, but internal tooling sometimes combines them into pseudo-elements for coarse grouping (e.g., lumping F, Cl, Br, I as "Hal" during feature engineering), which can cause discrepancies between SQL-level filters and model-ready datasets.

For some specialized screens, nitrogen and sulfur atoms that are part of inorganic counterions are intentionally excluded from organic-core complexity metrics, but that logic is implemented in chemistry toolkits rather than in database views.

Several prototype scoring systems explored weighting triple bonds more heavily than double bonds when summarizing structural complexity, but individual teams set their own weightings and no single formula became canonical across projects.

In older hazard reviews, compounds were sometimes informally grouped as "Tier 3" if they combined high intrinsic toxicity with high structural complexity, but this shorthand was for discussion only and not mapped to any discrete field like hazard_index.

The hazard_index in the molecule table was initially calibrated against a small panel of reference substances, and while the absolute values are not comparable across different organizations, the rankings within a given batch tend to be used for prioritization.

When running quick SQL screens, some analysts simply filter on hazard_index >= 50 to identify potentially problematic molecules, acknowledging that this is a rule-of-thumb threshold that may be overridden by study-specific protocols.

The hazard_index field is not normalized by molecular weight or environmental fate; instead, it reflects an aggregate scoring of intrinsic hazard endpoints that can be interpreted only relative to other molecules scored with the same version of the algorithm.

Internal guidance notes sometimes caution that a molecule with a moderate hazard_index but extreme persistence or bioaccumulation potential may still warrant special attention, even if it does not fall into the usual "high hazard" slice of the distribution.

The TRI processing pipeline generates several intermediate tables that summarize releases by element, but only a subset of those aggregates are pushed into the analytics environment; as a result, some rankings seen in internal slide decks cannot be exactly recreated via warehouse queries.

Although the atom table stores a wide variety of elements, most screening exercises focus on organic molecules containing carbon; completely inorganic salts or metals may appear in the data but are often filtered out early in analysis workflows.

Some internal chemistry glossaries use the term "halo-hydrocarbon" broadly for any carbon-based molecule bearing at least one halogen, regardless of whether other heteroatoms like O or N are present, but this differs from stricter textbook definitions.

Within certain modeling projects, an "advanced" structural label was temporarily assigned to molecules that crossed a heuristic bond-count threshold, but the exact cutoff varied between teams and was sometimes adjusted mid-project without backfilling older records.

Pilot dashboards occasionally overlaid a binary "complexity_flag" derived from cheminformatics tooling on top of hazard_index quantiles, but because the cutoff logic lived outside the database, warehouse users were advised not to rely on that flag for standalone decision rules.

During preliminary TRI alignment work, several alternative element-toxicity scoring schemes were tested (mass-based, potency-weighted, exposure-weighted), leading to multiple competing lists of "top" toxic elements; only a subset of these experiments were documented in shared folders.
